source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
In a vertical plane, a fixed circular insulating track has its center at O, with a radius $r = 0.5 \, \mathrm{m}$ and smooth inner walls. Points A and B are the lowest and highest points of the circular track, respectively. Within this region, there exists a uniform electric field directed horizontally to the right. A charged small ball (which can be treated as a point particle) with mass $m = 0.4 \, \mathrm{kg}$ and an absolute charge $q = 3 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{C}$ is able to remain stationary at point C. If the ball is given an initial velocity at point C such that it can just complete a full circular motion along the inner side of the track (with the charge of the ball remaining unchanged), it is known that points C, O, and D lie on the same straight line, and the angle between this line and the vertical direction is $\theta = 37^\circ$. The gravitational acceleration is $g = 10 \, \mathrm{m/s}^2$, and the position of point C is taken as the zero potential point for both gravitational potential energy and electric potential energy ($\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$, $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$).

1. Find the magnitude of the electric field strength; do not add any other content.
2. Find the minimum mechanical energy of the ball during its circular motion.

image_dependency: The problem refers to labeled geometric points A, B, C, O, and D on a circular track and an angle between line COD and the vertical, which is typically diagram-supported spatial information.
An image asset is registered and exists in image_manifest.
multi_step: The question has two explicit subproblems: first determine the electric field strength, then determine the minimum mechanical energy during motion.
Solving requires combining equilibrium information at point C with circular-motion conditions for 'just complete a full circular motion'.
The setup mixes geometry, force balance, and energy analysis, indicating multi-stage reasoning rather than direct lookup.
verifiability: The provided answer is numerical for both subparts, which is amenable to rule-based or symbolic checking.
Given explicit constants (r, m, q, g, sin 37°, cos 37°), the derivation can be independently recomputed.

decision_reasons: question, image, and answer are all present, so the core semantics are well supported
asset registry passed with no recorded issues
initial scores are consistently high for image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability
although the problem is diagram-dependent, the required diagram asset exists
the two-part numeric answer format remains verifiable and does not by itself block candidacy

If the ball is given an initial velocity at point C such that it can just complete a full circular motion along the inner side of the track (with the charge of the ball remaining unchanged), it is known that points C, O, and D lie on the same straight line, and the angle between this line and the vertical direction is $\theta = 37^\circ$. The gravitational acceleration is $g = 10 \, \mathrm{m/s}^2$, and the position of point C is taken as the zero potential point for both gravitational potential energy and electric potential energy ($\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$, $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$).

1. Find the magnitude of the electric field strength.
2. Find the minimum mechanical energy of the ball during its circular motion.

open_ended_problem_variants.title: PhysReason 开放题
open_ended_problem_variants.rewritten_question_text:
In a vertical plane, a fixed circular insulating track has its center at O, with a radius $r = 0.5 \, \mathrm{m}$ and smooth inner walls. Points A and B are the lowest and highest points of the circular track, respectively. Within this region, there exists a uniform electric field directed horizontally to the right. A charged small ball (which can be treated as a point particle) with mass $m = 0.4 \, \mathrm{kg}$ and an absolute charge $q = 3 \times 10^{-5} \, \mathrm{C}$ is able to remain stationary at point C. If the ball is given an initial velocity at point C such that it can just complete a full circular motion along the inner side of the track (with the charge of the ball remaining unchanged), it is known that points C, O, and D lie on the same straight line, and the angle between this line and the vertical direction is $\theta = 37^\circ$. The gravitational acceleration is $g = 10 \, \mathrm{m/s}^2$, and the position of point C is taken as the zero potential point for both gravitational potential energy and electric potential energy ($\sin 37^\circ = 0.6$, $\cos 37^\circ = 0.8$).

1. Find the magnitude of the electric field strength.
2. Find the minimum mechanical energy of the ball during its circular motion.
